narration.text: You have documentation, a codebase, a blog post, or just an idea. And you want to turn it into something people actually engage with. That's what the interactive video skill does. It takes any source and produces a self-contained HTML page with narrated canvas animations, quizzes, and interactive widgets. No video files, no hosting dependencies. Just one HTML file and a few supporting assets.

Let's look at what you actually get. The output is a single HTML page that loads a canvas-based animation engine. There's no video element at all. Instead, a JavaScript engine draws each scene frame by frame on a canvas, synchronized with narrated audio segments generated by edge text-to-speech. Quizzes pause the timeline and show an overlay. Widgets like sliders, sort interactions, and categorize panels let the learner do things, not just watch. Everything runs locally, offline, from a file URL.

The skill accepts four types of source input. First, a URL. The skill fetches the page, extracts the content, and optionally searches for supplementary context. Second, a file path. It reads the file and researches any frameworks or concepts it references. Third, the keyword codebase. The skill explores your repo with Glob, Grep, and Read, building understanding of patterns and architecture. Fourth, a plain topic string. It researches the topic in parallel using multiple agents, then extracts the natural structure from what it finds.

Before generation begins, you configure the lesson through an interactive configurator page. It runs locally in your browser. You pick the topics to include from the extracted list, choose your audience level, set the lesson length from quick to deep, select a visual theme from eight options, and pick a narration voice with live audio previews. The configurator outputs a structured preferences block that drives everything downstream. This is the only point where the skill asks for your input. After you paste the preferences, it runs to completion without stopping.

The generation pipeline has four phases, and the order matters. Phase one, Parse Preferences. The skill reads your configurator output and extracts audience, length, interaction style, theme, voice, and selected topics. Phase two, Structure. The skill classifies your content by knowledge type, decides what to include, sequences it using the epitomize, elaborate, summarize pattern, and assigns the right modality to each piece. Phase three, Generate. It writes a content dot JSON file with all narration text and interaction data, kicks off audio generation in the background, then writes the HTML page with scene definitions and canvas elements while audio builds. Phase four, Finalize. Once audio completes, the skill reads the computed timing, patches the HTML with real start and end times, validates everything, and serves the result.

Now let's look under the hood at the engine architecture. The output directory has a clear structure. At the root sits your lesson HTML file. Under src, there's a shared lesson dot CSS file and an engine folder with five JavaScript modules. Lesson dot JS handles boot, quiz overlays, and scoring. Player dot JS manages playback, timeline scrubbing, and chapter navigation. Audio dot JS provides a text-to-speech cascade, loading MP3 files first, falling back to browser speech synthesis. Canvas dot JS handles DPI scaling and exposes the VW and VH virtual coordinate constants. And scene renderer dot JS draws both declarative elements and custom animations.

The content dot JSON file is the data backbone of every lesson. It has three sections. The meta block sets the lesson ID, voice, speech rate, gap between audio segments, and total duration. The narration array holds one object per audio segment with a start time and the spoken text. When a narration segment should trigger an interaction, it includes an endsBeforeIx field linking to the interaction ID. And critically, the interactions field is an object keyed by ID, not an array. The engine looks up each interaction by its ID string. This is one of the most common mistakes when writing content dot JSON by hand.

Every scene on the canvas is defined twice. Once in the HTML as a JavaScript object with start time, end time, label, and a background key. And once in the scene elements map, which holds an array of declarative drawing instructions for that background key. The engine supports text, rect, rule, glow, and pipeline elements out of the box. Each element can have a revealAt value between zero and one, telling the renderer when within the scene's progress to fade it in. This creates the progressive reveal effect where content appears as the narrator mentions it.

For scenes that need more than static elements, you write a custom draw function. The signature is context, progress, time. All coordinates use VW and VH, the virtual width and height constants from canvas dot JS. These map to a nineteen twenty by ten eighty virtual space regardless of the actual screen resolution. Never use context dot canvas dot width or height directly, because those return DPR-scaled pixel values that break on retina displays. You can mix declarative elements and custom draw in the same scene. The renderer handles both.

Now for the interactive parts. The skill supports six interaction categories. Quiz, marked as cat Q, shows three options with single-letter badges. Each distractor represents a named misconception, and the feedback explains why the answer is right or wrong. Sort, cat try with widget sort, lets the learner drag items into the correct sequence. Categorize, also cat try, has the learner drag chips into labeled buckets. Slider lets them adjust a value and see contextual descriptions at different stops. Hotspot shows clickable items that must all be explored before the learner can continue. And code, cat code, displays a snippet with just a Finish button.

Finally, let's talk about themes. The skill ships with eight visual themes, each defined in styles dot JSON. Every theme provides the same set of CSS custom properties. Background, card, text, accent, green, warm, cool, and red colors. Font stacks for sans-serif, monospace, and serif. The HTML page sets these as root CSS variables, and the engine and lesson dot CSS consume them everywhere. This means you never hardcode colors in your scene elements. You pull from the theme palette. That way, the same lesson content can render correctly across Chalkboard's dark green, Terminal's warm amber, Notebook's cream paper, Blueprint's drafting blue, or any of the other themes.

That's the full picture. The interactive video skill takes any source, runs it through a four-phase pipeline of parsing, structuring, generating, and finalizing, and produces a self-contained HTML walkthrough with narrated animations and interactive exercises. The engine is modular, the themes are swappable, and the content format is declarative. Start with a URL, a file, or just a topic, and you'll have an interactive lesson in minutes.

try-source-types.title: Classify the Source Types
try-source-types.desc: Drag each example into the right input category.

categories.label: URL
items: A blog post about React hooks

correct.title: Correct!
correct.body: URLs fetch remote content, file paths read local files, and 'codebase' triggers full repo exploration with Glob, Grep, and Read.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: URL fetches a web page. File path reads a single local file. Codebase explores the whole repository.

try-phase-order.title: Order the Pipeline Phases
try-phase-order.desc: Drag to arrange from first to last.

correct.title: That's it!
correct.body: Parse drives Structure, Structure drives Generate, and Finalize patches in the real timing after audio completes.

wrong.title: Check the order
wrong.body: The pipeline flows: Parse → Structure → Generate → Finalize. Each phase depends on the output of the previous one.

q-interactions-type.title: Content Schema Check
q-interactions-type.desc: In content.json, how is the 'interactions' field structured?
options: An object keyed by interaction ID
A flat list of question strings

correct.title: Exactly!
correct.body: The engine looks up interactions by ID: CONTENT.interactions[ix.id]. Using an array is the most common mistake.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: Interactions is an object keyed by ID, not an array. The engine does CONTENT.interactions[ix.id] to find each one.

try-interaction-explore.title: Explore Interaction Types
try-interaction-explore.desc: Tap each type to learn when to use it.

hotspots.label: Quiz (cat: q)
hotspots.explanation: 3 options, each distractor is a named misconception. Use when there's a common misunderstanding to surface.

hotspots.label: Sort (cat: try)
hotspots.explanation: Drag items into correct order. Best for 4-7 items where sequence matters. Works great for procedures and pipelines.

hotspots.label: Categorize (cat: try)
hotspots.explanation: Drag chips into labeled buckets. Best for 2-3 categories with 3-5 items each. Use when things look similar but differ.

hotspots.label: Slider (cat: try)
hotspots.explanation: Adjust a value, see contextual feedback at stops. Needs at least 3 stops with distinct descriptions to feel worthwhile.

hotspots.label: Hotspot (cat: try)
hotspots.explanation: Clickable items that all must be explored before continuing. Gates progress. Best for systems with parts to map.

hotspots.label: Code (cat: code)
hotspots.explanation: Shows a code snippet with just a Finish button. Use sparingly for reference material the learner should study.

correct.title: All explored!
correct.body: Each type fits a different learning need. The design guide's modality table helps you pick the right one.

wrong.title: Keep exploring
wrong.body: Tap all the items to learn about each interaction type.

q-theme-vars.title: Theme Best Practice
q-theme-vars.desc: Why should you use CSS variables from styles.json instead of hardcoding colors?
options: Hardcoded colors render slower on canvas
CSS variables enable theme switching without changing scene code
The engine rejects hardcoded hex values

correct.title: Right!
correct.body: Same content, different theme. Pull from the palette and your scenes work across Chalkboard, Terminal, Notebook, and all the rest.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: The engine doesn't reject hex values, and performance isn't the issue. Theme variables let the same scene code render correctly across all eight themes.
